ThingDef.label: AN/M14 Incendiary Grenade
ThingDef.description: The Mk.3 A2 is an American concussion grenade.

ThingDef.label: M7A3 CS Grenade
ThingDef.description: The M7A3 is an American riot control grenade that is filled with 2-chlorobenzalmalononitrile, more commonly known as CS gas. The M7A3 uses the same mixture as the earlier M7A2 variant but the CS in the M7A3 is pelletized instead of being stored as gelatin capsules. The gas released by the grenade is intended to debilitate and incapacitate unprotected targets without causing lethal harm.

ThingDef.label: M34 Smoke Grenade
ThingDef.description: An American white phosphorous (sometimes called "Willy Pete") smoke grenade.

ThingDef.label: M67 Frag Grenade
ThingDef.description: The M67 is an American fragmentation grenade developed in the 1960s as the successor to the earlier M26/M61 and Mk.2 series of grenades. It is often called a "baseball grenade" because of its shape, which allows it to easily be thrown.

ThingDef.label: M69 Practice Grenade
ThingDef.description: The M69 is a practice/training variant of the American M67 fragmentation grenade. It contains no fuse or explosives, but otherwise has the same user controls and weight as the M67, making it an safe teaching aid for operating and throwing the real thing.

ThingDef.label: M83 Smoke Grenade
ThingDef.description: An American smoke grenade developed in the 1990s. The M83 was originally designated as a training aid, but later became a general-issue smoke grenade for use in combat.

ThingDef.label: M84 Stun Grenade
ThingDef.description: The M84 is an American stun grenade.

ThingDef.label: Mk.3 A2 Concussion Grenade
ThingDef.description: The Mk.3 A2 is an American concussion grenade.

ThingDef.label: M72A4 LAW
ThingDef.description: Caliber: 66mm LAW. 1-round tube. Single Use. A portable one-shot unguided anti-tank weapon. Produced beginning in 1963, the LAW was an effective replacement for both the rifle grenades and bazookas used in the anti-tank role up until that point. The M72 is extremely cost effective to produce and very portable, making it a popular platform even in the present.\nThis is the M72A4 variant, which features a more powerful rocket.

ThingDef.label: M72A7 LAW
ThingDef.description: Caliber: 66mm LAW. 1-round tube. Single Use. A portable one-shot unguided anti-tank weapon. Produced beginning in 1963, the LAW was an effective replacement for both the rifle grenades and bazookas used in the anti-tank role up until that point. The M72 is extremely cost effective to produce and very portable, making it a popular platform even in the present.\nThis is the M72A7 variant, which features an improved blast effect.

ThingDef.label: FIM-92J Stinger
ThingDef.description: Caliber: 70mm FIM-92. 1-round tube. Single-Shot. Can only be used against hostile buildings.

ThingDef.label: M60 RIS
ThingDef.description: Caliber: 7.62x51mm NATO. 100-round bullet link. Select-Fire. The M60 is an American belt-fed general purpose machine gun (GPMG) designed in the 1950s. It was conceived as part of an effort to replace both the M1918 BAR (which was fairly lightweight and portable, but had a very small magazine capacity) and the Browning M1919A6 (which fed from high capacity belts, but was extremely heavy and bulky) with a universal machine gun that shed the disadvantages of both platforms. Mechanically, the M60 was highly inspired by the German MG-42 and FG-42, and it bares a strong physical resemblance to both weapons. The M60 became an iconic weapon during the Vietnam War, where it earned the nickname "the Pig." This version has an aftermarket railed upper receiver installed.

ThingDef.label: SIG MG-338
ThingDef.description: Caliber: .338 Norma Magnum. 100-round bullet link. Select-Fire.

ThingDef.label: Mk.43 Mod 0
ThingDef.description: Caliber: 7.62x51mm NATO. 100-round bullet link. Select-Fire. The M60 is an American belt-fed general purpose machine gun (GPMG) designed in the 1950s. It was conceived as part of an effort to replace both the M1918 BAR (which was fairly lightweight and portable, but had a very small magazine capacity) and the Browning M1919A6 (which fed from high capacity belts, but was extremely heavy and bulky) with a universal machine gun that shed the disadvantages of both platforms. Mechanically, the M60 was highly inspired by the German MG-42 and FG-42, and it bares a strong physical resemblance to both weapons. The M60 became an iconic weapon during the Vietnam War, where it earned the nickname "the Pig." The Mk.43 Mod 0 is the US Navy designation for a variant of the M60E4, itself an update of the M60E3.

ThingDef.label: DTV XR338
ThingDef.description: Caliber: .338 Norma Magnum. 1000-round belt link. Select-Fire.

ThingDef.Name: CGF1_MWP_Badlands_Bowie
ThingDef.label: Badlands Bowie
ThingDef.description: A special tactical combat knife. For whatever reason, this design is particularly suited for counter-terror operations, and finds a lot of use by OpFor as well.

ThingDef.label: M7 bayonet
ThingDef.description: An American bayonet and knife based on the earlier M4 bayonet, designed for the M16 series of rifles. It was first adopted in 1964.

ThingDef.Name: CGF1_MWP_M9_Bayonet
ThingDef.label: M9 bayonet
ThingDef.description: An American bayonet and knife with a 7-inch (18 cm) blade. Designed by Charles Albert Finn of Phrobis III Ltd, the M9 bayonet was intended to be a dual-purpose utility knife and fighting bayonet for the M16 series of rifles. It was formally adopted in 1986, and has remained the standard-issue bayonet for the US Army ever since.

ThingDef.label: M11 EOD knife
ThingDef.description: The M11 EOD knife is a specialized variant of the M9 bayonet meant for Explosive Ordnance Disposal (EOD) purposes. Unlike the M9 bayonet, it has no mounting catch or muzzle ring - therefore, it cannot be used as a proper bayonet.

ThingDef.label: OKC-3S bayonet
ThingDef.description: A bayonet adopted by the United States Marine Corps (USMC) in 2003 to replace the earlier M7 and M9 bayonets.

ThingDef.label: Ontario Mark III Navy
ThingDef.description: A fighting knife produced by the Ontario Knife Company. It was adopted by the US Navy in the 1970s and has long-since been issued to US Navy SEALs.

ThingDef.label: SEAL 2000
ThingDef.description: A fighting knife that was acquired by the US Navy SEALs in the 1990s.

ThingDef.label: M14 RIS
ThingDef.description: Caliber: 7.62x51mm NATO. 20-round detachable mag. Semi-Automatic. An American gas-operated battle rifle descended from the M1 Garand. Desiring a universal platform that could replace the capabilities of the Garand (an infantry rifle), the BAR (a squad support weapon), and the Grease Gun (a close quarters combat weapon) in a single weapon, without any of the perceived drawbacks or limitations of such weapons, the Army began a series of trials. Modified M1 Garands were among the weapons submitted and tested, as was the FN FAL, but the resulting M14 rifle was controversially accepted and adopted in 1957, with issue beginning in 1959. However, the M14 would be replaced only a few short years later by Eugene Stoner's AR-15 design in the M16; nonetheless, M14s and its many derivatives would continue to be employed even 60 years after its adoption, ironically in the marksman/sniper role that it was not originally intended for. Fitted with an aftermarket rail interface system (RIS).

ThingDef.label: M2010 ESR
ThingDef.description: Caliber: .300 Winchester Magnum. 5-round detachable mag. Bolt-Action.

ThingDef.label: Mk.13 Mod 0
ThingDef.description: Caliber: .300 Winchester Magnum. 3-round internal mag. Bolt-Action. The Mk.13 is a series of bolt-action sniper rifles that were used by US Navy SEALs (and later, the Marine Corps) starting in the 1990s. All rifles in the Mk.13 series are built off of a Remington 700 long action and are chambered in .300 Winchester Magnum. There are eight rifles in the series (Mod 0-7) with each variant having modifications to the chassis, action, and barrel. The Mod 0 rifle uses a McMillan A2 chassis, and the Mod 1 rifle is identical except for its left-handed action. The Mod 2 rifle used an Accuracy International AICS chassis instead. The Mod 0/1/2 were later updated with suppressor-ready barrels, with the updated Mod 0 becoming the Mod 3, the Mod 1 becoming the Mod 4, and the Mod 2 becoming the Mod 5. The Mod 6 and Mod 7 rifles switched to a new Accuracy International AX chassis. There were likely never any left-handed versions of the Mod 2/5/6/7.

ThingDef.label: Mk.21 PSR
ThingDef.description: Caliber: .338 Lapua Magnum. 10-round detachable mag. Bolt-Action.

ThingDef.label: XM2010 ESR
ThingDef.description: Caliber: .300 Winchester Magnum. 5-round detachable mag. Bolt-Action.
